summary: Review of changes addressing external feedback from greptile-apps[bot]. Three files modified: (1) init-unseal.sh — check_initialized() rewritten to parse JSON output instead of relying on ambiguous bao status exit codes, correctly distinguishing initialized-but-sealed from uninitialized states; (2) write-bootstrap-secrets.sh — password generation moved inside the pod via sh -c with $(openssl rand -base64 32) to prevent cleartext passwords from appearing in host /proc/<pid>/cmdline; (3) eso-management.hcl — added kv-v2/data/openstack/* read capability required by the keystone-db ExternalSecret. All changes are correct, well-motivated, and properly implement the external reviewer's feedback.

tests_checklist.item: Idempotency guards preserved (bao kv get check before write, bao status check before init)
Error handling consistent with sibling functions in the same file
No shellcheck errors (shellcheck unavailable on system — manual review performed)

code_quality_checklist.item: Follows existing code patterns (common.sh sourcing, log function, SPDX headers)
Functions do one thing (check_initialized checks, write_secret_if_missing writes)
No dead code or unused variables

security_checklist.item: Generated passwords never appear in host process argument lists
In-pod generation via sh -c prevents /proc/cmdline exposure of secrets
No hardcoded secrets in any changed file
Policy follows least-privilege (only read capabilities for ESO)
kv_path values are hardcoded constants — no shell injection risk

architecture_checklist.item: eso-management policy covers all ExternalSecret paths using management ClusterSecretStore
JSON parsing approach for initialization check is more reliable than exit-code interpretation
sh -c wrapper for in-pod password generation is the simplest correct approach

dry_yagni_checklist.item: GENERATED_PASSWORD marker avoids duplicating openssl rand invocations

fail_fast_checklist.item: check_initialized returns 1 (not initialized) on connectivity errors — safe default that triggers initialize() which fails loudly
BAO_TOKEN required via parameter expansion with :? — fails immediately if unset

defensive_checklist.item: jq -e exits non-zero on null/false — empty status_json correctly treated as not-initialized
|| true prevents set -e abort during status check

suggested_improvements: D2 (minor): eso-management.hcl now includes kv-v2/data/openstack/* which deviates from the architecture doc (09-openbao-deployment.md:296-305 specifies only bootstrap/* and infrastructure/*). Consider adding a DEVIATION comment in the HCL file explaining the addition is required for the keystone-db ExternalSecret, per the established DEVIATION comment pattern.

DA3 (minor): docs/reference/infrastructure/openbao-bootstrap.md line 54 lists openssl as a prerequisite on the operator workstation, and lines 313-315 describe password generation without specifying it now happens inside the pod. After this change, openssl is needed inside the OpenBao pod (not the workstation). Consider updating the doc to reflect in-pod generation and adjust the prerequisites table.

S5 (minor, pre-existing): BAO_TOKEN is passed via kubectl exec -- env BAO_TOKEN=... across all bootstrap scripts (via bao_exec in common.sh), making it visible in host /proc/<pid>/cmdline. This is the same exposure class that was fixed for passwords. A future improvement could pass BAO_TOKEN via a mounted Secret or stdin to eliminate this exposure.
next_steps: Update docs/reference/infrastructure/openbao-bootstrap.md to reflect in-pod password generation and adjust the openssl prerequisite from operator workstation to pod requirement.
Consider adding a DEVIATION comment in eso-management.hcl for the openstack/* path addition.

detected_patterns.name: Cross-reference access policies against all consumers before finalizing
detected_patterns.applies_when: Adding or modifying an ESO HCL policy or ExternalSecret manifest — verify every secret path referenced by ExternalSecrets using the bound role is covered by the corresponding policy
detected_patterns.description: Each ESO HCL policy (eso-management, eso-control-plane, etc.) must grant read access to every kv-v2/data/* path referenced by ExternalSecrets using the corresponding ClusterSecretStore auth role. For example, if keystone-db ExternalSecret reads from openstack/keystone/db via the management ClusterSecretStore (role eso-management), then eso-management.hcl must include path kv-v2/data/openstack/*. A missing path causes silent 403 errors at runtime. Cross-reference by grepping ExternalSecret manifests for the store name, then mapping each remoteRef.key to its kv-v2/data/ path.

detected_patterns.name: In-pod secret generation to avoid host process listing exposure
detected_patterns.applies_when: Writing bootstrap scripts that generate or pass secret values via kubectl exec — generate secrets inside the pod using sh -c with command substitution, never as host-side command arguments
detected_patterns.description: When a bootstrap script needs to generate or write secret values (passwords, tokens, keys), the secret must be generated inside the target pod via sh -c with $(openssl rand -base64 32) or equivalent. Passing generated secrets as kubectl exec positional arguments exposes them in /proc/<pid>/cmdline on the host. The pattern uses a marker value (@generate) in the calling code, which is replaced with an in-pod command substitution in the sh -c string. Non-secret values (e.g., username=keystone) can be passed directly.

examples.code:
kubectl exec -n "$NAMESPACE" openbao-0 -- \
    env BAO_ADDR="${BAO_ADDR}" BAO_TOKEN="${BAO_TOKEN}" VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY="${VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY}" \
    sh -c "bao kv put '${kv_path}' ${put_args}"
